item.title: 在雪中一次充满意外挑战的春季远足
item.description:
About two weeks ago I went for a hike on a mountain that we hadn’t gone since quite some time. Last year we tried a new route to the summit but didn’t do the full strecth for some reasons. Since then I wanted to go this route again, just this time, up to the summit. Just during the winter I didn’t dare to go there for safety reasons. But then, two weeks ago it had been warm long enough that I expected most of the snow being gone on all the south facing parts. On the very top, I had to follow a ridge where I exepected still some snow in the shade, but I thought I could give it a try.

That said I drove to the trailhead and started my tour. Luckily I was prepared for warmer temperatures but I was still surprised by the relavitely high temperatures (~10-15°C). The trail itself was beautiful, exactly as expected: the first flowers were already visible and brought some color into the brown landscape. The summits of the surrounding higher mountains were still covered in snow but the signs of spring were inevitable. Unfortunately, my legs felt a bit sore right from the beginning. I had hoped that this would go away after a while of walking but it didn’t. So I just went a little slower so that I was in a convenient pace and not suffering from my tired legs.

When I reached the ridge, it didn’t take long until I hit some snow, just as I had expected. After I sank deep into the snow for the first time, so that I had some snow in my boots, I decided to put on gaiters and microspikes. It totally paied off bringing them along! During the ~1.3km, I would say 2/3 were snow covered and the gaiters saved me from loading quie some snow into my boots!

At the summit, I cleaned up some litter, took a short rest to have a snack and enjoyed the view before I headed back the same route. Indeed I didn’t spend too much time there as a couple of other people came along and took their spot quite next to me. Sure, I could have moved but I didn’t feel like I wanted to spend much more time there anyways.

On the way back, I had an unpleasant surprise. At one step I just heard a metallic “pling” sound – and a quick look at my microspikes confirmed: a metall plate had broken … sigh. Again … it feels like something like this happens every couple of years. Just that a metal plate breaks? That was new. Well they were still usable, at least, but a topic for contacting the vendor back home.

Anyways, I just walked on until at one spot, I suddenly broke into the snow with my left leg – hips deep. For a second I paniced a bit “how should I get out of this alone?!” But also immediately calmed down. There was another hiker about 100m behind me, it was no super dangerous spot (well quite a slope at my side but sill) and I should be able to just get out somehow. The snow was hard enough to be able to crawl out. And it was another situation where I was just very glad to wear gaiters!

The hiker offered some assistance – which was not needed, fortunately and after getting myself sorted again, I walked on. But I just had learned that snow can indeed be deceptive. I didn’t assume that it would be so deep at that spot and that it was just soft enough to sink in so far. Later on, I lost the intended track when I just followed the footsteps in the snow and didn’t notice that they forked off the main “trace”. Another learning: in the snow, you don’t see the real trail, just footsteps of people who were ahead. Luckily it wasn’t a big deal again and I just walked parallel to the main trail until I came back to it.

The way down was pleasantly uneventful. Close to 20°C in the sun, beautiful views, another adventure in my memories and some learnings as well.

item.title: 一场差点没能成行的冬日徒步
item.description:
Due to several reasons I hadn’t found the opportunity to do some snowshoeing this winter season. Yet, last weekend was a weekend after a couple of colder days and snowfall. And I thought that this might be one of the last opportunities I could get to get my feet into deep snow. I mean: of course, higher up, the moutains are still quite white. But you also have to get up there first. And even then, it would be nice to walk in descend climb and not very steep. I’ve walked some steep sections in snow shoes in th past and it’s … moderate fun. Especially when the snow is wet and heavy.

The evening before I thought about where to go: I didn’t want to drive too far, I didn’t want to go to one specific mountain again that I just had walked twice recently, I didn’t want to take ANY risc in terms of avalanches, some views would be nice and there should be a parking space where I was sure that it was cleared from snow (I had some bad surprises with this very fact in the past!). Actually a very nearby region did not come to my mind but was told me just later by a friend! So I decided to go to an area that I had snow shoed already last winter and that held quite fond memories.

This time, I also decided that I’d like to record some video footage as I hadn’t done a real video (and well, I recorded enought for a video).

Well. As you can see in the video, there was a bit amount of snow! Sometimes about 30cm, higher up it was more. But the snow shoes were really worth their weight! Actually I just wanted to just walk until the house seen in the video. But as conditions were just very good and I wasn’t the first one to walk there (which always makes it ways easier), I felt strong enought, I just continued to hike higher up.

After a short while I noticed that I started catching up to a group of three snowshoers. Unfortunately, I was a bit faster so they let me pass and suddenly the “trail” was only prepared by ski tourers. At some point I forked off their trace as they crossed a slope which I didn’t find very convenient. I made my – more snowshoe friendly – way up to the spot where I that the summer trail was below the snow. Walking in untouched snow is definitely fun – but some fun that is paied in energy 😀 But it was worth every step.

Later on, I rejoined the ski tourer-trace and hiked higher up. At this part, the path got ways steeper. That was actually the reason why I didn’t even plan to go that far. And the fact that I probably wouldn’t have gone if there were no trace at all. But with the trace ahead, I felt confident to walk on. This section is also more in the shade of the mountain so I joined a real snowy wonderland: Frozen branches, lot’s of (now not wet) snow, silence all around. I noticed that I had missed that all winter long, which made it even more joyful to me.

After about three hours overall, I checked the map and saw that about 70m elevation were still ahead until I would reach a plain. Yet these 70m would be even a bit steeper and I wondered if the view from up there would be worth going up. I knew alreadythat I would not do the last 50m eleveation to the summit as those were really steep. I guess they would be doable, but I was going alone and my risk-tolerance in winter is very close to zero.

I put off my rucksack, grabbed my bottle, took a sip and thought: “ways not enough to drink”. Well okay, uncomfortable but not dangerous. I didn’t plan to go that far … exactly. Anyways – I looked back to where I came from – and to where I would go back now. This is always a strange moment to me. I know, how long it took me and that the way back is less tiring. But still I’m always impressed by the way back. – But it doesn’t help, what comes up must come down again.

And so I started my way back through the snow. A couple that I met during the way up (and with whome I had some nice chats), returned at the same point. There’S not much to say about my way back. Ecept that it was a bit more sunny (and thus bright). I was really glad that I had my sunglasses with me!

After about 5h I was back at the car. Feeling a bit sore in the legs and back, and super happy about my decision to go there.

item.title: 冬末春初的徒步之旅
item.description:
嘿，亲爱的读者！我目前正遭受着典型的“没有足够的业余时间来追求我所有的爱好”的困扰！过去几天（嗯，也许是几个晚上），我都在忙我的一个副业项目。不幸的是，一天只有24小时，所以我没能抽出时间来讲述我们最近的冒险经历。但这并不意味着我们没有出门。

比如前一个周末！天气预报说天气异常温暖，阳光明媚，我们想尝试一座主要朝南的山，以避免泥泞和积雪，不能太高，但也不能只是散散步，还有其他一些我们想要满足的条件……我承认，我通常会遇到一个问题：总是想寻找新的、特别的、冒险的东西……但尤其是在冬天或临近冬天的时候，这通常是不可能的，或者只是愚蠢的想法。经过一番讨论，我们决定去一座我们熟悉的山区，计划爬升约670米（约2000英尺）。

我知道，春天已经近在咫尺了！

几周前我去过同一个地区。当时，我被那里的积雪量和阴凉处的寒冷气温吓了一跳。为此，我们准备了夹克放在背包里，穿上了长袖……结果出发后不久我们就后悔了！哇，我们真是低估了太阳的威力。很快，我们就希望自己只穿了T恤。我有个优势：穿的是拉链裤，所以很快就能把裤腿拉下来。别误会，并不是一直都很热。只是在阳光下，温度比“舒适”稍微热了一点，我们很高兴能偶尔走进阴凉处。

周围的景色——嗯——就像一年中这个时候的样子：相当枯黄。不过，第一批花已经出现了！而且我已经在一些灌木上看到了第一批嫩芽。这让我微笑，因为我知道春天已经近在咫尺了！当其他一些徒步者从我们身边经过继续往上走时，我们短暂休息了一下，因为我想捕捉枯黄死草中那些星星点点的色彩。——关注我博客的人都知道，对我来说重要的不是山顶，而是沿途的所有体验。看到这些春天的迹象绝对属于其中。

出于某种原因，这座山是我们最喜欢的之一：上山的路非常多样。你不会总是走在相同环境、相同类型的路线上。你从一条宽阔的林间道路开始，很快就变成了一条小径。有较陡的路段，也有更平坦好走的路段。有些部分沿着小草地延伸，有一次我们穿过一片小山毛榉树林——这是我很喜欢的一段。有些部分沿着陡峭的斜坡走，有些则是缓坡……

一路上总是有那些类似石楠的花。我总惊讶于已经有足够的昆虫来为它们授粉。尤其是在一年中的这个时候，天气的变化可能意味着几天内就能用30-50厘米的积雪掩埋一切。但这就是自然：必须有某些昆虫和植物来预示春天的到来；总得有人做第一个……正如你在照片中看到的：我们选择这条路线是个相当好的决定，因为我们没有踩到任何雪或泥。大部分路段都是干燥好走的。

就在快到山顶前，我们又短暂休息了一下，把自己裹好：我把腿套（如果这个词对的话）重新系到裤子上，又加了一层衣服，因为山顶没有树，风可能很大。——就像今天这样！山顶上还残留着冬天的痕迹，有些雪地，但说实话没什么值得拍照的。

我们在那里待了一会儿，吃了点零食，欣赏了全景，拍了几张照片。过了一会儿，我们俩都觉得开始变冷了，想下山了。于是，我们收拾好东西，开始下山，很高兴能摆脱冷风。嗯，剩下的路程并不特别壮观——我们看到了同样美丽的景色，只是享受一起在户外的时光。

就这样……我希望你也喜欢和我们一起在山里的感觉，期待很快再次见到/读到你的消息！

item.title: 周日漫步迎春
item.description: 一次沿伊萨尔河畔的即兴漫步证明，即便是平凡的出行也能带来惊喜。远处霜雪覆盖的山峰与春天的初声——绿草、几朵小花——让我们将壮阔的远足换成了静谧的时光。有时，最好的冒险源于无心之举，而最简单的风景反而最令人难忘。

item.title: 又一次雪地徒步，天空蓝得不真实
item.description:
I haven’t been hiking too much lately. The landscape is still bare, and I don’t enjoy treks that start in wet snow with temperatures hovering just above freezing. Snow can provide grip, but only if it’s firm. Otherwise, microspikes become a necessity. Yet even those have their limits, when the snow clumps to the micro spikes.

Yet, at the end of January, I decided to try a south-facing route. Webcams showed the north side still buried in snow, but I hoped the sun would have cleared the southern flank. As I drove to the trailhead, I saw that my plan had some flaws. The valley was still blanketed in snow. I clung to the hope that the forest trail might be clear or at least well-trodden.

Well .. I found the parking lot frozen solid. The decision of wether or not to use micro spikes was an easy one and I mounted them right from the beginning. The trail was steep, with countless steps, and snow still covered parts of it. But I wasn’t the first to walk this path, and the packed snow made for easy going. The microspikes gave me confidence, and I never hesitated about not having enough grip.

The ascent itself was unremarkable. I didn’t take a photos until I emerged from the forest. I wasn’t particularly eager to capture the scene, but I wanted to show you how it looked. The blue of the sky was so vivid it almost seemed unreal. I had to desaturate it slightly in post-processing — otherwise, it would have looked fake. But that’s nature for you sometimes!

By the way, the contrast was .. weird. At the base of the mountain, snow was everywhere. The higher I climbed, the more the sun took over, until there was no snow at all on the sunlit slopes. Then, just as I stepped onto the north-facing side, the snow returned. I had expected to move int o more or into less snow – not that it was changing a couple of times. But well … that was a learning, too. Fortunately, I wasn’t the first to venture this way. I followed the path, enjoying the view back toward the valley, and tried to capture a few impressions of the snow-covered landscape.

After leaving the side of the mountain, the terrain flattened out. That’s when I nearly regretted not bringing my snowshoes. In several spots, the snow was deep enough to make walking uncomfortable. But I went on, scouting the area for views and checking the snow conditions on the other side of the mountain. I had considered a few alternative routes that morning but wasn’t sure what to expect, so I was eager to see what the conditions were there.

I even stumbled upon a spot I hadn’t known before. It was beautiful, but I failed to take a decent photo. Maybe next time. The snowshoes would have helped there, too. But carrying them all the way up just for half an hour of easier walking? I’m still not sure it would have been worth it.

For the return trip, I adjusted my route slightly, hoping to capture a particular shot. It didn’t turn out as impressive as I had imagined, but I still want to share it with you. Along the way, I found patches where the snow was over half a meter deep. Luckily, I managed to keep it out of my shoes—even though I had forgotten to wear gaiters.

The descent was unspectacular. I chatted with a group of young hikers who asked about the trail and whether I thought trekking poles were worth using. I had been using them, and one of them was curious – he had seen others with poles but wasn’t sure if they made a difference.

After a pleasant discussion, we went our separate ways. I headed home with fond memories and a few new photos on my memory card.
